pages.text:
Preface
In veterinary texts, discussion of guinea pig medicine and surgery has previously been confined 
to chapters or sections in exotics or rodent books. All too often discussion of guinea pig medicine 
is coupled with rabbit medicine or welfare. While guinea pigs do share many issues with these 
other species they are far from being short-eared rabbits and are distinctly different from other 
rodent species kept as pets.
In addition there has been growth in guinea pig keeping and in owner commitment to these 
engaging and interesting pets. This may be part of the general rise in pet keeping during 
Covid-19 lockdowns, or because people have become more aware of the charms of guinea pig 
keeping, or also for legal reasons (e.g. in parts of the world where rabbit keeping is not allowed).
The result of this has been a corresponding rise in guinea pig numbers presented to veterinary 
clinics by owners who no longer consider guinea pigs as ‘pocket’ or ‘children’s’ pets but more 
as a fully integrated family member. With this commitment has come an increased desire for 
accurate diagnoses, better treatment, and much less ‘It’s only a…’.
In short, knowledge has increased and it is certainly time for a dedicated text on guinea pig 
medicine. Showing that this increased interest in keeping and treating guinea pigs is a global 
change, the editors have been able to draw together authors from many different countries and 
continents to produce this first BSAVA Manual devoted to cavies.
Yet there are still significant issues within guinea pig keeping and their medicine – we are 
becoming more aware of their welfare issues and that keeping methods have not completely 
kept pace with increased commitment. As with many fields of veterinary medicine, old and new, 
there is a relative lack of evidence-based clinical information, giving rise to a range of opinions 
and controversies, which the editorial team have tried to include to allow readers to make their 
own decisions. These debates must be opened out and new evidence gathered – any future 
editions of this text will likely be very different!
As well as introducing the range and depth of possibilities in diagnosing and treating guinea pigs, 
the editors have tried not to lose sight of the purpose of a BSAVA Manual – that it should be a 
practical, easy-to-use resource for the practising veterinary surgeon. There are occasions when 
the ideal treatment plan is not possible and a more contextualized approach is appropriate. The 
Manual therefore also covers the basics and where, when and how to take a more pragmatic 
approach.
In short, this Manual covers guinea pig medicine and surgery from husbandry and nutrition, 
through diagnostic testing and on to medicine and surgery. In achieving this the editors are 
extremely grateful to all the authors for sharing their commitment and knowledge of guinea pigs. 
They would especially like to thank the BSAVA Publications team for all their help and support in 
producing this Manual and, especially, for sharing the editors’ vision that this Manual is needed. 
And finally the editors would like to thank their families for their patience, understanding, and 
tolerance of yet more absenteeism while they worked on this project.
John Chitty
Deborah Monks
Anna Meredith
Bob Doneley
November 2025
Delivered by BSAVA to:
viii  Jeffrey Chan (40591)
